graph.confusable_reason: Both produce powdery, rusty pustules (uredinia) on wheat leaves and are routinely confused in the field, especially early when pustules are sparse. They share an orange-to-yellow color range and both are foliar rusts of wheat, but they differ decisively in pustule arrangement (stripes vs. scattered) and temperature/season preference.
graph.shared_presentation: Small, raised, powdery rust-colored pustules erupting through the leaf epidermis on wheat foliage during the growing season, shedding orange-to-yellow urediniospores onto fingers and clothing, with chlorosis around lesions and reduced photosynthetic area leading to yield loss.

forks.question: What temperature regime and time of season is favoring the outbreak?
forks.a_signal: Cool conditions (50-60F), develops in early spring and at higher elevations/northern or coastal-influenced areas; suppressed by hot weather
forks.b_signal: Warmer conditions (60-77F), intensifies later in spring into early summer as temperatures rise

forks.question: On which tissues are pustules concentrated and how widely do they spread?
forks.a_signal: Pustules on leaves, leaf sheaths, glumes and awns; readily moves onto the head
forks.b_signal: Predominantly on leaf blades, sparingly on sheaths; rarely on the head

forks.question: How are the pustules arranged on the leaf blade relative to the veins?
forks.a_signal: Yellow-orange uredinia arranged in narrow, dashed LINEAR STRIPES running parallel to the veins between the leaf veins
forks.b_signal: Round-to-oval orange-brown uredinia SCATTERED randomly across the blade with no linear pattern

forks.question: What is the precise color and size of the urediniospore mass?
forks.a_signal: Bright lemon-yellow to yellow-orange, smaller pustules
forks.b_signal: Orange to orange-brown (cinnamon/brick), slightly larger round pustules

forks.question: As the crop matures, where do telia (black overwintering pustules) form?
forks.a_signal: Black-brown telia form in linear stripes mirroring the earlier yellow striping, often on sheaths and glumes
forks.b_signal: Black telia form as scattered, mostly hypophyllous (underside) pustules without a striped pattern

graph.narrative_a: Stripe rust (yellow rust) is the cool-season wheat rust. Look for bright lemon-yellow to yellow-orange uredinia arranged in distinct narrow stripes running lengthwise between the veins of the leaf blade, often extending onto sheaths, glumes, and awns. It establishes early in spring under cool, moist, often overcast conditions and at higher elevations or in northern and Pacific-influenced regions, and it declines once daytime temperatures climb into the 70s-80s F. The striped arrangement and yellow hue are the giveaways; on the head, striping on glumes confirms it.
graph.narrative_b: Wheat leaf rust (brown rust) is the warmer-season counterpart. Pustules are round to oval, orange to cinnamon-brown, and scattered randomly across the upper leaf blade with no linear organization, concentrated on blades rather than sheaths or heads. It builds later in the season as temperatures warm into the 60s-70s F. The absence of striping, the more brick-orange color, and the random distribution distinguish it from stripe rust; black scattered telia appear on leaf undersides at maturity.
graph.management_a: Plant resistant cultivars (race-specific and adult-plant resistance); scout early in cool spring weather. Apply triazole or strobilurin foliar fungicides at first detection, prioritizing protection of the flag leaf and head through flowering; track regional rust forecasts and spore-trapping alerts because new virulent races migrate.
graph.management_b: Use resistant varieties with effective Lr genes; monitor as temperatures warm into late spring. Foliar triazole/strobilurin fungicides at flag-leaf emergence to heading protect yield when pustules appear early; eliminate volunteer wheat that serves as the green bridge for overwintering inoculum.

lay.farmer_lay_report: My wheat leaves have small, raised, rust-colored bumps that erupt through the surface and rub off as an orange-to-yellow powder onto my fingers and clothes. The leaves are yellowing around the bumps and the plants look weaker, so I'm worried about losing yield.
lay.decisive_lay_a: Look for the bumps lined up in tight rows that run straight up and down the leaf, parallel to the long lines (veins) in the blade, like dashed yellow-orange stripes.
lay.decisive_lay_b: Look for round-to-oval orange-brown bumps scattered randomly all over the blade with no rows or stripe pattern.
